3 min read
Just now

--

When I built my first React project, my only goal was to make it work. If the page rendered without errors, I was happy. I didn’t care whether the code was clean or messy. But as my projects got bigger, I started spending more time understanding my old code than writing new features. That’s when I realized something:
Working code isn’t always good code.
Over time, I changed a few habits that made my React code much cleaner and easier to maintain. Here are the ones that helped me the most.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image generated by ChatGPTMistake to write everything in a one component
One of my biggest mistakes was putting everything inside a single component.Some files became 400–500 lines long. Whenever I wanted to fix a bug, I had to scroll endlessly to find the right section. Now, if a component starts getting too large, I split it into smaller pieces. Instead of one giant ProductPage, I create components like:

• ProductCard

• ProductImage

• ProductInfo

• ProductActions
Each component has one responsibility, and that makes the code much easier to understand. Earlier, my src folder looked like a storage room. Everything was mixed together. Now I separate files into folders like:

• components

• pages

• hooks

• context

• services

• utils
Finding a file now takes seconds instead of minutes.
In my early projects, I copied and pasted code everywhere. If I needed the same button on three pages, I copied it three times. Later I learned that React is all about reusable components. Now I create a component once and use it wherever I need it. It saves time and makes updates much easier.
Better name for variables
I used to write variables like:
const data = {};
const value = [];
const item = {};After a few weeks, I had no idea what those names meant. Now I try to choose names that explain the purpose.
For example:

• userData

• productList

• cartItems

• isLoading
Good names make the code almost self-explanatory.
Earlier, I wrote API calls directly inside components. As the project grew, the components became difficult to read. Now I keep API-related code inside a separate services folder. The component focuses on the UI, while the service handles the data. That small change made my code much cleaner.
This might sound small, but consistent formatting makes a huge difference. I keep:

• Proper indentation

• Meaningful spacing

• Clear imports

• Organized JSX
It doesn’t change how the app works, but it makes the project much easier to read.
One lesson that took me time to learn was this, Your first solution doesn’t have to be your final solution. If I find a better way to organize a component, I refactor it. Improving old code is part of becoming a better developer.
the biggest Changing
The biggest difference wasn’t learning a new React feature. It was changing my mindset. Instead of asking,
“Does this code work?”
I started asking,
“Will this code still make sense to me after three months?
That one question completely changed the way I write React applications.
